label: Neural Coding
definition: The study of how neurons represent and transmit information through their patterns of electrical activity, whether by firing rate, precise spike timing, or the joint activity of populations. It is the question of what the brain's signals actually mean.

body.text: Neural coding asks the most basic question in systems neuroscience: how do the electrical signals of neurons carry information about the world? A neuron communicates through spikes, brief all-or-nothing voltage events, and the puzzle is which features of those spikes constitute the message. The field tries to crack the brain's representational language by relating stimuli and behavior to the activity they evoke, in both directions: encoding, predicting neural activity from a stimulus, and decoding, reconstructing the stimulus or intention from observed activity. The answer matters because everything the brain does, perception, memory, action, is carried in this code.

The oldest and most established hypothesis is rate coding, the idea that information lives in how fast a neuron fires, averaged over a short window. Edgar Adrian established in the 1920s that stronger stimuli drive higher firing rates, and rate codes remain a workhorse. But rate coding can be slow, because averaging needs time, and it discards the fine structure of when each spike occurs. Temporal coding holds that the precise timing of spikes, their latency, their order, their phase relative to ongoing brain rhythms, carries additional information that a rate average throws away. The two views are not exclusive, and much of the brain likely uses both, with timing mattering more where speed and precision are at a premium.

A further dimension is population coding, the recognition that meaning often resides not in any single neuron but in the joint activity pattern across many. A direction of motion or a planned reaching movement can be read out far more accurately from a population vector than from one cell, and population codes are naturally robust, degrading gracefully when individual neurons are noisy or lost. This distributed picture connects directly to other theories: it is how a bayesian-brain could represent a full probability distribution rather than a single value, and the correlations that population codes depend on are shaped by plasticity rules like hebbian-learning. It also marks where coincidence and timing become computational, which is the regime the spiking-neural-network is built to exploit.

The relevance to artificial intelligence runs in both directions. The distributed, population view of neural coding is the biological echo of the embedding, the dense vector in which modern models encode a word, an image, or a concept as a pattern across many dimensions rather than in one unit. Understanding which code the brain uses is also essential for building faithful spiking-neural-network models and for programming neuromorphic-computing hardware, where the choice between rate and temporal coding is a concrete design decision with direct consequences for energy and speed. And neural coding is the foundation of brain-computer interfaces, which must decode movement intentions from recorded activity in real time, a direct engineering test of how well we have cracked the code.
